_meta.note: book-quiz tone (퀴즈로읽는책) · curly apostrophes only · glossary locked

title_001: Our Twisted Hero — Dopamine Master Quiz · Yi Mun-yol
html_001: Yi Mun-yol · 1987 · Yi Sang Literary Award–winning political fable
html_002: A small dictatorship inside a classroom — a portrait of all of us standing before it
html_004: “You surrendered to an unjust power — and didn’t even know to be ashamed.”
html_005: — the new teacher’s rebuke, from the novel
html_006: Master Han Byeong-tae, Eom Seok-dae, and this novel’s people, events, and themes through
html_008:
.
        They start easy and get steadily harder,
html_009: and every one you miss comes back until you get it right.
html_014: the rise & fall of Seok-dae’s kingdom
html_026: That’s the end of the free preview
html_039: Our Twisted Hero at a Glance
html_041: ‘the rise and fall of a kingdom’
html_042: — thread it all onto that one idea and you’ll never forget it. It all starts with
html_043: a transfer into a strange classroom
html_046: 🚸 From Seoul to a rural classroom
html_047: Han Byeong-tae thrown into a strange kingdom
html_050: An order woven from violence, tribute, and rigged exams
html_053: Privilege and comfort — the sweet payoff of conforming
html_055: 🧑‍🏫 The new teacher arrives and exposes it all
html_056: Rigged exams uncovered · a rebuke that wakes them up
html_059: “The submissive crowd turns on a dime — and the tears”
html_061: Table of Contents at a Glance
html_063: 3 Cases from the Book Worth Pondering
html_065: Focused Review of What You Missed
html_067: lets you explore the literary significance of Our Twisted Hero and the world of Yi Mun-yol in far more depth.
html_073: A repeat master quiz that turns a book’s core into dopamine
html_074: This app is secondary study content that reorganizes the book’s core ideas for learning purposes. For the full reading experience, please turn to the original book.

js_004: Which Korean author wrote Our Twisted Hero?
js_009: Our Twisted Hero is a novella Yi Mun-yol published in 1987. It won the Yi Sang Literary Award that very same year — not the year after.
js_011: What’s the name of the protagonist who transfers from Seoul to a rural school?
js_016: The protagonist is Han Byeong-tae — the observer who carries the whole story, and the narrator looking back on it all as an adult.
js_018: What’s the name of the class monitor who rules the class with absolute power?
js_023: The one who rules the class is monitor Eom Seok-dae — a model student to the teacher, a tyrant to the kids. Two faces in one.
js_025: Where does most of this novel take place?
js_026: A classroom in a small-town elementary school
js_027: A lecture hall at an elite Seoul university
js_029: A lighthouse out by the sea
js_030: The stage is a small-town elementary classroom. All the politics of power and submission are compressed into one cramped room.
js_032: What brings Han Byeong-tae to the rural school?
js_033: His civil-servant father’s demotion and transfer
js_034: He was forced to transfer after causing big trouble
js_035: His parents divorced and left him on his own
js_036: He needed time to recover from an illness
js_037: Byeong-tae moves from Seoul to the countryside after his civil-servant father is demoted. The gap between glamorous Seoul and the shabby countryside is where the story begins.
js_039: What position did Eom Seok-dae hold in the class?
js_044: Seok-dae’s role is class monitor. This small bit of power, handed down by the teacher, becomes the tool he uses to rule the entire classroom.
js_046: Which era is this novel set in?
js_047: The late 1950s, end of the Liberal Party regime
js_048: The 1930s, deep in Japanese colonial rule
js_050: In the middle of the Korean War
js_051: The backdrop is the late 1950s, the end of the Liberal Party regime. The mood right before the April 19 Revolution seeps into the classroom story.
js_053: Which best describes how Eom Seok-dae ruled the other kids?
js_054: Forcing submission through violence and intimidation
js_055: Leading with fair rules and votes, always
js_056: Winning goodwill by handing out money
js_057: Always looking out for the weak first
js_058: Seok-dae wrings submission out of the kids through violence and coercion. Orderly on the surface, the whole world is held together by fear underneath.
js_060: Who arrives in sixth grade and brings Seok-dae down?
js_062: The teacher from the next class over
js_065: The one who topples Seok-dae’s kingdom is the newly arrived sixth-grade teacher. A force from outside shakes an order that had hardened in place.
js_067: What does Han Byeong-tae do for a living as an adult?
js_072: The grown-up Byeong-tae is a cram-school instructor — an ordinary man, nothing special. The failures and compromises of his younger days linger in the way he carries himself.
js_081: Encyclopedia of Korean Culture — “Our Twisted Hero”
js_082: Yi Mun-yol (b. 1948) was a defining figure of Korean literature in the 1980s and ’90s. Our Twisted Hero won him the 1987 Yi Sang Literary Award.
js_083: Han Byeong-tae is the transfer student from Seoul, and the narrator. The story flows through his eyes as he observes, resists, and finally gives in.
js_084: Eom Seok-dae is a tyrant who rules the class through the power of the monitor’s office — a model student before the teacher, an absolute ruler before the kids.
js_085: The stage is a single classroom in a small-town elementary school. Into this cramped space, the birth, upkeep, and collapse of power are packed whole.
js_086: Byeong-tae transfers from Seoul to the countryside after his civil-servant father is demoted. The gap between the dazzling city and the shabby village plants the seed of conflict.
js_087: Seok-dae’s post is class monitor. This small authority handed down by the teacher grows into a tool of control that reaches into violence, tribute, and rigged exams.
js_088: The setting is the late 1950s, the twilight of the Liberal Party regime. The air just before the April 19 Revolution reads right over the classroom story.
js_089: Seok-dae draws out submission through violence and force. Orderly on the outside, the world is kept running by fear within.
js_090: The new sixth-grade teacher brings Seok-dae’s kingdom down. A force from outside is the decisive spark that shakes an order gone rigid.
js_091: The grown-up Byeong-tae is a cram-school instructor, an unremarkable ordinary man. The setbacks and compromises of his youth have soaked into the way he stands.
js_105: A tyrant who rules the class through the monitor’s power. A model student to the teacher, an absolute ruler to the kids — the twisted hero himself.
js_106: The transfer student from Seoul, and the narrator. Resisting, isolated, and finally surrendering — a wavering self-portrait of the intellectual.
js_108: The birth, upkeep, and collapse of power are compressed into one rural elementary classroom. This cramped space is a whole society, a whole nation.
js_110: The secret behind Seok-dae’s top rank. Each subject’s best student erased their own name and turned in the answer sheet under his. Organized cheating that propped up his power.
js_112: The kids gave up snacks and nice things and bowed down. Small tributes and silence pile up until the tyrant’s kingdom is complete.
js_113: Arriving in sixth grade, the new teacher exposes Seok-dae’s fraud and pushes the kids to wake up. A force of awakening from outside — a revolutionary spark.
js_115: The moment his authority cracks, the kids trip over each other to denounce Seok-dae. He smashes a window and vanishes, and the crowd’s two-facedness comes to light.
js_117: An allegory of dictatorship and conformity that borrows a classroom for its stage. The shadows of the late Liberal Party era and April 19 overlap, keeping the questions about power and the crowd alive to this day.
js_120: Yi Mun-yol’s signature novella, published in 1987 and winning that year’s Yi Sang Literary Award.
js_121: A political fable staged in a single rural elementary classroom.
js_122: Set against the late-1950s twilight of the Liberal Party, just before April 19.
js_123: A frame narrative: the grown-up Han Byeong-tae looking back on the past.
js_125: A Transfer, and a Strange Kingdom
js_126: His civil-servant father’s demotion moves Byeong-tae from Seoul to the countryside.
js_127: The class was already under monitor Eom Seok-dae’s absolute rule.
js_128: Byeong-tae is stunned by an order woven from violence, tribute, and submission.
js_129: He resolves to stand alone against the injustice.
js_132: With classmates and teacher alike on Seok-dae’s side, Byeong-tae is utterly isolated.
js_133: He shouts for justice, but no one takes his side, and the loneliness wears him down.
js_134: In the end he drops his resistance and folds into Seok-dae’s order.
js_135: The privileges and comfort of an inner-circle member — the sweet reward of giving in.
js_138: A new teacher arrives in sixth grade and looks at the classroom with fresh eyes.
js_139: The rigged exams — top students turning in answer sheets under Seok-dae’s name — come to light.
js_140: The teacher scolds them for surrendering to injustice without even knowing to feel ashamed.
js_141: Once his authority collapses, the kids scramble to denounce Seok-dae.
js_144: Seok-dae smashes a window, leaves the school, and disappears.
js_145: Facing the crowd that turned on a dime, Byeong-tae feels more bitterness than triumph.
js_146: Years pass, and Byeong-tae grows into an ordinary, unremarkable adult.
js_147: On a trip, he witnesses Seok-dae being hauled away in handcuffs.
js_149: The Twisted Hero, a Portrait of Us
js_150: It re-examines the two-facedness of a crowd that swings between submission and betrayal.
js_151: Byeong-tae, who resisted and then gave in, mirrors the intellectual inside all of us.
js_152: Dictatorship and conformity — the shadow of April 19 reads over the classroom.
js_153: An ending that closes on tears he can’t explain leaves a long aftertaste.
js_154: The Swapped Name on the Exam
js_155: A kid is caught erasing his own name at the top of the exam and writing in Seok-dae’s. The moment the secret behind Seok-dae’s top rank — the rigged exams — is exposed.
js_156: It shows that his power rested on organized cheating, not ability — and it pulls the trigger on the kingdom’s collapse.
js_158: The new teacher rebukes the kids fiercely: you were robbed and didn’t know to feel wronged, you gave in and didn’t know to feel ashamed.
js_159: It wakes them to how silence and submission were themselves complicity — the novel’s key line, turning the question of responsibility back on a conforming crowd.
js_161: The grown-up Byeong-tae, on a trip, sees Seok-dae being led away by a detective. The once-tyrant, hauled off in a sorry state, seen from behind.
js_162: It shows how power ends, and through Byeong-tae’s unexplained tears, it reopens the question of a past never truly settled.
js_163: Inside an unjust order, would I be the one who resists — or the one who joins in and enjoys the comfort?
js_164: Think of Byeong-tae’s surrender. The sweetness of privilege is a frightening temptation for anyone.
js_165: What does it mean that Seok-dae was brought down not by the crowd itself, but by the new teacher?
js_166: An order overturned by an outside force can, at any moment, slide into yet another kind of submission.
js_167: How should we see a crowd where yesterday’s loyal servants become today’s accusers?
js_168: Consider how conformity and betrayal can be one and the same — in the light of Byeong-tae’s bitterness.
js_170: You read Our Twisted Hero perfectly
js_172: You caught almost all of the core
js_174: You’ve got the framework — one more round!
js_176: The more you repeat, the higher your score

_app.desc: Read a small classroom dictatorship through a 30-question quiz
